coredata.dc:title: Modernization of charcoal drugs: Integrating research paradigms of carbon dots to gain new perspectives
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Tianjin Press of Chinese Herbal Medicines. Published by ELSEVIER B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: Tianjin Press of Chinese Herbal Medicines. Published by ELSEVIER B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Sichao
Hu, Zhanglu
Zhang, Weidong
coredata.dc:description: With the modernization drive of traditional Chinese medicine (TCM), this perspective innovatively proposes integrating carbon dot research paradigms to facilitate the modernization of charcoal drugs in TCM. The research focuses on five core areas: analyzing and validating charcoal drugs components pharmacologically, exploring modern preparation methods, establishing a quality evaluation system, fixing preparation process parameters, and probing into the material basis. These steps aim to deepen the scientific understanding of the material basis of charcoal drugs, optimize its preparation process, and establish a comprehensive quality control system. This work provides a theoretical foundation and experimental evidence for the scientific understanding of charcoal drugs, further promoting their modernization and internationalization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: carbon dots
charcoal drugs
material basis
modernization
quality evaluation system
standardization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Charcoal drugs: Wisdom of TCM and modern innovative challenges
Carbon dots (CDs): A bright star in field of nanotechnology
Similarities between CDs and charcoal drugs
Integrating carbon dots research paradigms to facilitate modernization of charcoal drugs exploration
Analysis of charcoal drugs components and pharmacological validation

National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Weidong Zhang is a distinguished professor of the Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and a professor of the School of Pharmacy, Second Military Medical University, China. He has devoted himself to the research and development of new traditional Chinese medicine, the material basis and mechanism of action of traditional Chinese medicine compound. He has published more than 500 papers, and has been cited more than 4 800 times. A total of 48 domestic invention patents have been authorized, seven international invention patents have been applied, and three have been authorized. Three new drug certificates were obtained. Zhang was honored the National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars, the Qi Huang Scholar Support Program, the Chang Jiang Scholars Program of China, the Leading Talent of Technological Innovation of Ten-Thousands Talents Program, and the Special Government Allowances of the State Council. He has won two second prizes of National Science and Technology Progress Award and two first prizes of Shanghai Science and Technology Progress Award. He was the vice chairman of the Natural Medicine Committee of the tenth Chinese Pharmacopoeia Commission and the vice chairman of the Chinese Medicine Chemistry Committee of the World Federation of Chinese Medicine Societies.

This work was supported by CAMS Innovation Fund for Medical Sciences (CIFMS) (No. 2023-I2M-3-009), Key Project at Central Government Level: The Ability Establishment of Sustainable Use for Valuable Chinese Medicine Resources (No. 2060302-2305-02), Postdoctoral Fellowship Program of CPSF (GZC20230316), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2023M740330, 2024T170078) and Young Scientists Fund of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82405031).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Interrelationship of charcoal drugs and carbon dots.

simple-head.ce:title: Modernization of charcoal drugs: Integrating research paradigms of carbon dots to gain new perspectives

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Medicinal Plant Development, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College, Beijing 100193, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Medicinal Plant Development, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College, Beijing 100193, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Second Military Medical University, Shanghai 200433, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Second Military Medical University, Shanghai 200433, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the modernization drive of traditional Chinese medicine (TCM), this perspective innovatively proposes integrating carbon dot research paradigms to facilitate the modernization of charcoal drugs in TCM. The research focuses on five core areas: analyzing and validating charcoal drugs components pharmacologically, exploring modern preparation methods, establishing a quality evaluation system, fixing preparation process parameters, and probing into the material basis. These steps aim to deepen the scientific understanding of the material basis of charcoal drugs, optimize its preparation process, and establish a comprehensive quality control system. This work provides a theoretical foundation and experimental evidence for the scientific understanding of charcoal drugs, further promoting their modernization and internationalization.

ce:sections.ce:para: In recent years, the development of modern science and technology has brought new opportunities to traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) research. Advanced analytical techniques like high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), along with computational methods, have enabled TCM to move towards standardization and internationalization. However, charcoal drugs, a significant part of TCM, encounter obstacles in their modernization. Their pharmacological material basis remains unclear, and the lack of consistent quality evaluation systems and diverse preparation methods pose challenges. To overcome these issues and promote the modernization of TCM, this perspective innovatively proposes integrating carbon dot research paradigms to study charcoal drugs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charcoal drugs: Wisdom of TCM and modern innovative challenges
ce:section.ce:para: Charcoal drugs represent TCM processed through high-temperature firing techniques, imparting them with special pharmacological effects such as hemostasis, antidiarrheal action, astringency, and detoxification, while also reducing the toxicity of some medicinal materials. Charcoal drugs are widely utilized in the field of TCM, particularly renowned for its hemostatic properties. Examples include Crinis Carbonisatus and Sanguisorbae Radix Carbonisata, both of which are used to treat hemorrhagic conditions, embodying the traditional theory of “blood stops when it encounters blackness.” Additionally, charcoal drugs possess various efficacies such as astringing the intestines to stop diarrhea and moderating medicinal properties. For instance, Mume Fructus Carbonisata is used to treat dysentery (Li et al., 2022), while Zingiberis Rhizome Carbonisata effectively removes irritants from dried ginger (Zingiber officinale Roscoe) (Chen et al., 2024). However, the traditional preparation process of charcoal drugs is relatively crude, lacking scientific rigor and standardization. Furthermore, insufficient research has been conducted on its pharmacological mechanisms and material basis, which to some extent constitutes a critical bottleneck restricting the modernization and internationalization of charcoal drugs. Additionally, the active ingredients in charcoal drugs are complex and diverse, posing significant challenges for accurate and effective isolation and identification, thereby greatly impacting the quality control and pharmacological evaluation of charcoal drugs. Consequently, deeply revealing the active ingredients and their pharmacological mechanisms in charcoal drugs, optimizing its preparation process, and establishing a scientific and comprehensive quality evaluation system have become the key focus and core tasks of current efforts to promote the modernization of charcoal drugs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots (CDs): A bright star in field of nanotechnology
ce:section.ce:para: CDs, as a class of nanocarbon materials synthesized through various methods, including high-temperature sealed calcination, possess a rich variety of surface functional groups and excellent biocompatibility. Their initial discovery dates back to 2004 (Xu et al., 2004). Based on their basic structures, CDs can be categorized into four types: graphene quantum dots with a two-dimensional layered graphene core, carbon quantum dots with a spherical crystalline core, carbon nanodots with an amorphous core, and carbonized polymer dots featuring a highly dehydrated cross-linked polymer framework or a slightly graphitized core (Ðorđević, Arcudi, Cacioppo, & Prato, 2022). A notable characteristic of CDs is their ability to retain certain inherent properties of their raw materials while acquiring new features and functionalities. Furthermore, their exceptional optical properties, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and chemical stability render them broad application potential and prospects in multiple disciplines such as biomedicine, photoelectric conversion, and photocatalysis (Zhao et al., 2022). In the field of biomedicine, CDs have already been utilized in cancer treatment, inflammation therapy, hemostasis and analgesia, cellular imaging, and drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Similarities between CDs and charcoal drugs
ce:section.ce:para: The pioneers of quantum dot research were awarded the Nobel Prize in chemistry in 2023. This prestigious award emphasizes the importance of quantum dots in promoting the development of different technologies and inspiring more research in this interesting field. Carbon quantum dots are carbon based nanomaterials with unique properties and are a branch of CDs. As research on CDs and charcoal drugs deepens, researchers have gradually become aware of the close correlation between the two. Firstly, from the perspective of preparation temperature, the synthesis process of CDs is highly similar to the processing technique of charcoal drugs. CDs are most commonly synthesized under sealed conditions at temperatures ranging from 90 to 300 °C (Liu et al., 2024), while charcoal drugs are processed at temperatures approximately between 80 and 250 °C. This similarity suggests that there may be structural and property similarities between these two. Secondly, in terms of structure, the element with the highest content in CDs is carbon, with part of the carbon atoms carbonized to form the core, while some side chains of the original material molecules remain on the surface. Similarly, carbon is also a crucial element in charcoal drugs, where part of the herbal material is burned to a charcoal-black color during processing, while another part retains its original morphology. Lastly, during the preparation of CDs, precise control over the preparation conditions, such as raw material selection, reaction temperature, and time, enables fine-tuning of the size, shape, and surface chemical properties of the CDs, resulting in reduced toxicity and the acquisition of new functions and properties, thereby broadening their application fields.

In recent years, several publications have reported that TCM charcoal drugs and their extracts, when subjected to high-temperature carbonization, can produce “nano-sized charcoal drugs particles” with particle diameters smaller than 20 nm (Zhao, Qu, & Zhao, 2022). The preparation process of these particles exhibits similarities to the synthesis method of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), leading to their categorization as “Chinese medicine-derived carbon quantum dots.” These TCM-derived CQDs not only possess hemostatic and anti-tumor properties but also exhibit low toxicity, good water solubility, high biocompatibility, and superior photoluminescence performance. Yan et al. successfully isolated CDs with hemostatic effects from charcoaled Pollen Typhae (Yan et al., 2017) and CDs for the treatment of acute kidney injury (Wang et al., 2023). Furthermore, Lonicerae Japonicae Flos-derived CDs demonstrate anti-inflammatory effects and have the potential to alleviate fever and hypothermia caused by inflammation (Wu et al., 2020).

After conducting an in-depth analysis of the similarities between CDs and charcoal drugs in terms of preparation temperature, preparation process, structural characteristics, biological toxicity, and performance features, and synthesizing existing research findings, this perspective hypothesizes that CDs and charcoal drugs may play pivotal roles in interconnecting and bridging applications within the biomedical field. Based on the aforementioned hypothesis, an innovative viewpoint was proposed, which suggests the potential interoperability between the research methodologies of CDs and charcoal drugs. One of the important active substance bases in charcoal drugs is CDs, and the standardized preparation methods and characterization techniques for CDs may be reversely applied to the standardized processing and quality evaluation system of charcoal drugs. They can serve as bridges and ties between each other, facilitating mutual understanding and application expansion (Fig. 1 ). If this innovative viewpoint is scientifically validated, it will not only significantly enhance the scientific nature, social recognition, and clinical application scope of charcoal drugs research but also provide new solutions and practical pathways for addressing the many inconsistencies currently existing in the construction of quality evaluation systems and the standardization of processing techniques for charcoal drugs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integrating carbon dots research paradigms to facilitate modernization of charcoal drugs exploration
ce:section.ce:para: This perspective prospectively proposes a systematic research framework aimed at elucidating the carbon dot components within charcoal drugs, optimizing preparation techniques, and establishing a scientific quality evaluation system. The objective is to deepen the understanding of carbon dot components in charcoal drugs, refine their preparation processes, and establish a scientific quality management and evaluation system. The specific research contents can be summarized into the following five core aspects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of charcoal drugs components and pharmacological validation
ce:section.ce:para: Utilizing advanced technological means, precise separation and characterization of various sizes of carbon dot components within charcoal drugs are conducted. Subsequently, cellular and animal experiments are employed to verify their pharmacological efficacy and safety, laying a theoretical foundation for the clinical application of charcoal drugs.

Prepare charcoal drugs based on the established and recognized preparation standards of CDs. By strictly controlling the preparation parameters, a comparison of the pharmacological differences between self-prepared and commercially available charcoal drugs is conducted, aiming to optimize the preparation process and enhance the pharmacological efficacy and stability of charcoal drugs.

Drawing on the characterization techniques of CDs, combined with methods such as fluorescence spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy, a quality evaluation system for charcoal drugs is constructed to ensure consistency in their quality and therapeutic effects.
By fixing the preparation process, the preparation efficiency and consistency of charcoal drugs are improved, providing solid technical support for the industrial production of charcoal drugs.

The correlation between the structure of CDs and the core components of charcoal drugs is investigated, encompassing elements, element distribution, size, charge, surface groups, etc. The influence of different preparation times, temperatures, and methods on the pharmacological effects/mechanisms of CDs is explored. Combining pharmacological experimental results with preparation process parameters, the interactions between CDs and other active ingredients in charcoal drugs, as well as their pharmacological mechanisms, are thoroughly discussed.

This series of steps are closely interconnected and mutually supportive, collectively constituting a complete and systematic research framework designed to advance the research on charcoal drugs and open up new paths for their modernization and international development.

The core of this perspective lies in innovatively constructing a methodological bridge between CDs and charcoal drugs. It aims to deepen the scientific understanding of carbon dot components in charcoal drugs and to facilitate the optimization and upgrading of charcoal drugs preparation processes, as well as the establishment and improvement of a quality control system. By integrating research paradigms of CDs to promote the modern exploration of charcoal drugs, this research not only reveals the potential carbon dot components and their pharmacological effects in charcoal drugs, optimizes the preparation processes, and establishes a scientific quality evaluation system, but also provides a theoretical basis and experimental evidence for the clinical application of charcoal drugs. Furthermore, the ultimate goal of this research is to advance the modernization of charcoal drugs research and offer new pathways and ideas for the international development of charcoal drugs.

Sichao Tian: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Zhanglu Hu: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Weidong Zhang: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by CAMS Innovation Fund for Medical Sciences (CIFMS) (No. 2023-I2M-3-009), Key Project at Central Government Level: The Ability Establishment of Sustainable Use for Valuable Chinese Medicine Resources (No. 2060302-2305-02), Postdoctoral Fellowship Program of CPSF (No. GZC20230316), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2023M740330, 2024T170078) and Young Scientists Fund of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82405031).

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chen et al., 2024

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zingiberis Rhizoma-based carbon dots alter serumoestradiol and follicle-stimulating hormone levels in female mice

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, Y. M., Bai, X., Zhang, Y., Zhao, Y. F., Ma, H. G., Yang, Y. B., Wang, M. J., Guo, Y. H., Li, X. P., Wu, T., Zhang, Y., Kong, H., Zhao, Y., & Qu, H. H. (2024). Zingiberis Rhizoma-based carbon dots alter serumoestradiol and follicle-stimulating hormone levels in female mice. Artificial Cells, Nanomedicine, and Biotechnology, 52(1), 12-22.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ðorđević et al., 2022

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A multifunctional chemical toolbox to engineer carbon dots for biomedical and energy applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ðorđević , L., Arcudi, F., Cacioppo, M., & Prato, M. (2022). A multifunctional chemical toolbox to engineer carbon dots for biomedical and energy applications. Nature Nanotechnology, 17, 112−130.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Li et al., 2022

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Changes in chemical composition and medicinal effect of Mume Fructus before and after charcoal frying and its principle of hemostasis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, C. S., Xin, J. P., Wang, H. L., Xu, W. J., Song, X. H., Ye, X. W., Zhang, H. X., Li, Q., & Li, X. R. (2022). Changes in chemical composition and medicinal effect of Mume Fructus before and after charcoal frying and its principle of hemostasis. Chinese Traditional and Herbal Drug, 53 (24), 7714−7720.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of carbon dots in synthesis strategy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu, H. X., Zhong, X., Pan, Q., Zhang, Y., Deng, W. T., Zou, G. Q., Hou, H. S., & Ji, X. B. (2024). A review of carbon dots in synthesis strategy. Coordination Chemistry Reviews, 498, 215468.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Wang et al., 2023

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasmall and highly biocompatible carbon dots derived from natural plant with amelioration against acute kidney injury

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, X. K., Wu, T., Yang, Y. X., Zhou, L., Wang, S. X., Liu, J. X., Zhao, Y. F., Zhang, M. L., Zhao, Y., Qu, H. H., Kong, H., & Zhang, Y. (2023). Ultrasmall and highly biocompatible carbon dots derived from natural plant with amelioration against acute kidney injury. Journal of Nanobiotechnology, 21, 63.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Wu et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of Lonicerae Japonicae Flos Carbonisata-derived carbon dots on rat models of fever and hypothermia induced by lipopolysaccharide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wu, J. S., Zhang, M. L., Cheng, J. J., Zhang, Y., Luo, J., Liu, Y. H., Kong, H., Qu, H. H., & Zhao, Y. (2020). Effect of Lonicerae Japonicae Flos Carbonisata-derived carbon dots on rat models of fever and hypothermia induced by lipopolysaccharide. International Journal of Nanomedicine, 15, 4139−4149.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Xu et al., 2004

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single‐walled carbon nanotube fragments

sb:maintitle.ce:italic: Journal of the American Chemical Society

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xu, X. Y., Ray, R., Gu, Y. L., Ploehn, H. J., Gearheart, L., Raker, K., & Scrivens, W. A. (2004). Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single‐walled carbon nanotube fragments. Journal of the American Chemical Society, 126(40), 12736−12737.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Yan et al., 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hemostatic bioactivity of novel Pollen Typhae Carbonisata-derived carbon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhao et al., 2022

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Narrow-bandwidth emissive carbon dots: Arising star in the fluorescent material family

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Research progress on pharmacological effects of nano-components of charcoal drugs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao, Y. S., Qu, H. H., & Zhao, Y. (2022). Research progress on pharmacological effects of nano-components of charcoal drugs. Chinese Traditional and Herbal Drugs, 53(3), 921−929.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Weidong Zhang is a distinguished professor of the Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and a professor of the School of Pharmacy, Second Military Medical University, China. He has devoted himself to the research and development of new traditional Chinese medicine, the material basis and mechanism of action of traditional Chinese medicine compound. He has published more than 500 papers, and has been cited more than 4 800 times. A total of 48 domestic invention patents have been authorized, seven international invention patents have been applied, and three have been authorized. Three new drug certificates were obtained. Zhang was honored the National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars, the Qi Huang Scholar Support Program, the Chang Jiang Scholars Program of China, the Leading Talent of Technological Innovation of Ten-Thousands Talents Program, and the Special Government Allowances of the State Council. He has won two second prizes of National Science and Technology Progress Award and two first prizes of Shanghai Science and Technology Progress Award. He was the vice chairman of the Natural Medicine Committee of the tenth Chinese Pharmacopoeia Commission and the vice chairman of the Chinese Medicine Chemistry Committee of the World Federation of Chinese Medicine Societies.
